EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated over the mountains of North Carolina during the afternoon hours. As the afternoon progressed, several supercell thunderstorms developed which tracked southeast across the foothills and piedmont along a slow moving surface cold front. Many of the storms moved along the North Carolina, South Carolina border. With unusually steep lapse rates over the region, several of the storms produced large hail. Fortunately the supercells were a little elevated in nature, which kept any tornadoes from forming over South Carolina. Still, hail ranging up to the size of softballs did quite a bit of damage over the region.

Thunderstorms initiated over the mountains of North Carolina during the afternoon hours. As the afternoon progressed, several supercell thunderstorms developed which tracked southeast across the foothills and piedmont along a slow moving surface cold front. Many of the storms moved along the North Carolina, South Carolina border. With unusually steep lapse rates over the region, several of the storms produced large hail. Fortunately the supercells were a little elevated in nature, which kept any tornadoes from forming over South Carolina. Still, hail ranging up to the size of softballs did quite a bit of damage over the region.

A short wave trough was a catalyst for convection just west of the forecast area in the late afternoon. The convection became organized and progressed toward the coast through the evening, with numerous boundary interactions taking place while this occurred. The airmass was unstable with steep low-level lapse rates and favorable DCAPE values supportive of strong downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter report nickel size hail on Bacons Bridge Road.

Thunderstorms initiated over the mountains of North Carolina during the afternoon hours. As the afternoon progressed, several supercell thunderstorms developed which tracked southeast across the foothills and piedmont along a slow moving surface cold front. Many of the storms moved along the North Carolina, South Carolina border. With unusually steep lapse rates over the region, several of the storms produced large hail. Fortunately the supercells were a little elevated in nature, which kept any tornadoes from forming over South Carolina. Still, hail ranging up to the size of softballs did quite a bit of damage over the region.
A squall line moved rapidly east to southeast from the mountains through the Midlands of South Carolina taking down numerous trees and powerlines and producing some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported many trees down in the western part of Fairfield county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid and upper level ridge produced large thickness values over the southeast. As the afternoon progressed, several supercell thunderstorms developed which tracked southeast across the foothills and piedmont along a slow moving surface cold front. Many of the storms moved along the North Carolina, South Carolina border. With unusually steep lapse rates over the region, several of the storms produced large hail. Fortunately the supercells were a little elevated in nature, which kept any tornadoes from forming over South Carolina. Still, hail ranging up to the size of softballs did quite a bit of damage over the region.

A short wave trough was a catalyst for convection just west of the forecast area in the late afternoon. The convection became organized and progressed toward the coast through the evening, with numerous boundary interactions taking place while this occurred. The airmass was unstable with steep low-level lapse rates and favorable DCAPE values supportive of strong downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A emergency manager reported limbs down and blocking the road near the intersection of Old Sheldon Church Road and Trask Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire was reported near Carolina Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning apparently started a fire near the community of Carolina Forest.  Ongoing hot temperatures and drought conditions allowed the fire to breach containment lines several times. The blaze consumed 1374 acres before being contained on July 29th. No homes were damaged or threatened in spite of the fire's close proximity to the Carolina Forest subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved out of northeast Georgia and into the western Upstate during the early evening hours. While the wind shear wasn't strong over the region, steep low level lapse rates resulted in a high downdraft CAPE (DCAPE) environment. As result, even as the line weakened considerably over South Carolina, it still blew down a number of trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Greenville-Spartanburg Int'l airport measured a wind gust of 58 mph, with a couple of trees blown down on the airport property. Downed trees and broken limbs continued across Lyman and into the city of Spartanburg. Trees and limbs were also down in the Inman and Boiling Springs areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moved out of northeast Georgia and into the western Upstate during the early evening hours. While the wind shear wasn't strong over the region, steep low level lapse rates resulted in a high downdraft CAPE (DCAPE) environment. As result, even as the line weakened considerably over South Carolina, it still blew down a number of trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and a few power lines were blown down in and near Westminster.  Multiple trees were also blown down along Coffee Rd to the southwest of Walhalla as well as in Walhalla. Several trees fell on homes and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated over the mountains of North Carolina during the afternoon hours. As the afternoon progressed, several supercell thunderstorms developed which tracked southeast across the foothills and piedmont along a slow moving surface cold front. Many of the storms moved along the North Carolina, South Carolina border. With unusually steep lapse rates over the region, several of the storms produced large hail. Fortunately the supercells were a little elevated in nature, which kept any tornadoes from forming over South Carolina. Still, hail ranging up to the size of softballs did quite a bit of damage over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of golf ball size hail fell from Old Mill Rd, across the east side of Gaffney, to a point about 6 miles southeast of Gaffney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold pool and outflow boundary over the Midlands in South Carolina during the late evening hours. Thunderstorms became strong as they entered an unstable atmosphere in Southeast South Carolina featuring MUCAPE between 3000-4000 J/KG, DCAPE between 1000-1500 J/KG, lifted indices between -6 to -7, and favorable low and mid-level lapse rates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An environment of strong wind shear and weak to moderate instability supported the development of a mini-supercell thunderstorm over the eastern Upstate of South Carolina. The storm produced an EF2 tornado in Chester County, along with a couple of weaker tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that touched down near Lowrys in extreme northern Chester County moved into York County southeast of McConnells, just east of Darby Rd. The damage path was non-continuous for the first three miles of its track across York County, although open farmland made it difficult to determine if the tornado was in constant contact with the ground in this area. Damage was generally confined to scattered downed trees. Damage intensified as the tornado approached the Williamson Rd area, near its intersection with Erby Rd, where tree damage became more concentrated. The tornado became very strong and increased in size as it approached and crossed highway 324 in the Ogden Community, near its intersection with Skyline Rd and Williamson Rd. Around a dozen homes were damaged in this area, with one double-wide mobile home being rolled about 75 yards. Two occupants of this home were killed. Another man was killed when the roof of his home was blown off, causing a chimney to collapse on him. Five other people were injured in the Ogden area. A number of sheds and outbuildings were damaged or destroyed and several automobiles were also flipped. The tornado lifted east of Skyline Rd, just short of Vernsdale Rd. The tornado was the strongest to hit York County in almost 40 years, and was the county's first known killer tornado since 1926. The thunderstorm that spawned the tornado was a mini-supercell that traveled over 110 miles before dissipating shortly after the second tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in the northeast Gulf of Mexico and tracked eastward across the northern Florida peninsula, then northeastward off the southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina coast.  Meanwhile, a shallow cold airmass remained in place in the lee of the Appalachians by high pressure north of the area, allowing a continued supply of cold and dry air at the surface.  The warm temperatures well above ground level and freezing or sub-freezing temperatures at ground level, resulted in freezing rain and ice accumulation across much of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down along Sniders Highway near the Hampton county border due to ice accumulation.  Ice accumulation was estimated to be around 0.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved through the Gulf of Mexico and across northern Florida producing snow, sleet and freezing rain across the southeast U.S.  Heavy snow fell across the Midlands of South Carolina with accumulations ranging from 1 inch in the southeast portions with up to 10 inches in the northwest portions.  The snow changed to sleet and then to freezing rain and drizzle with ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch.  Power outages were reported in the southern area of the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported snow accumulations of 1 to 2 inches in the north portion of the county.  Freezing rain followed the snow with ice accumulations of 1/2 inch causing many power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado initially touched down in a rural area of extreme northern Chester County, near the Lowrys community, just west of Darby Rd. The tornado traveled northeast, across Darby Rd before crossing into York County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS personnel confirmed a EF-1 tornado with estimated wind speeds between 105 to 110 miles per hour. A tornadic waterspout formed over northern Lake Moultrie in South Carolina at 503 PM and traveled east northeast for 2 miles before coming onshore 2.5 miles southwest of Russellville South Carolina. The tornado then traveled east northeast before dissipating 1 mile southeast of Saint Stephen, South Carolina. The tornado lifted a roof off a church, destroyed a mobile home, shifted two other mobile homes off their foundations, tore half the roof off a house, destroyed a barn, tore shingles and/or siding off of a dozen homes/mobile homes, uprooted or snapped off many hundred trees, damaged or destroyed sections of several fences and knocked down several power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front and moved through the area producing some large hail and wind damage.  Most of the damage was due to trees falling, some on homes or vehicles.  Three small tornadoes also touched down in the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey confirmed a HAM radio report of a tornado touchdown southwest of Ft. Motte.  An EF1 tornado with winds of near 100mph took down numerous trees and powerlines, damaged a farm, and rolled over a large section of a field irrigation system.  Nickel size hail was also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey confirmed an EF1 tornado of winds near 90 mph had intermittent touch downs along its path.  Numerous trees were either blown over or snapped.  Minor damage was done to several homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures in combination with low relative humidity values, resulted in conditions favorable for wildfire starts in southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Forestry Commission reported a 125 acre wildfire near Pineland Estates Road and Zig Zag Road, 3 miles northeast of Pineland, South Carolina.  The fire reportedly involved one home and a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures in combination with low relative humidity values and breezy conditions, resulted in conditions favorable for wildfire starts in southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Forestry Commission reported a large wildfire started on March 23 in northern Charleston County, 5 miles north-northeast of McClellanville, South Carolina.  The wildfire was fully contained on March 25.  The wildfire burned 2600 acres over the 3 day period, resulting in the destruction of 16 non-residential structures, evacuation of the Germantown and Santee Communities, the opening of a shelter at the St. James-Santee Elementary School, and the closure of Highway 17 from Highway 45 north to the Charleston/Georgetown County line.  Damages as a result of the wildfire were estimated between 1.5 and 2 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into the area after 8 pm ahead of a pre-frontal trough. These storms had a history of producing damaging wind gusts and remained severe as they moved into our westernmost counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team confirmed a microburst with winds up to 70 mph near Hartsville, South Carolina. The path length was about 0.3 miles and the average path width was 0.1 miles. The microburst downed or snapped a dozen trees along Sun Valley Drive. One of these trees did fall onto a house causing some damage to the roof. The metal roof and siding of a large storage shed were stripped and then blown onto a house causing damage to the roof of a house on Ousleydale Road. Six trees were also down in this area and blocking the road. A car accident reportedly resulted when a vehicle encountered a large pine tree across Ousleydale Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into the area after 8 pm ahead of a pre-frontal trough. These storms had a history of producing damaging wind gusts and remained severe as they moved into our westernmost counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wires were reportedly down at a convenience store on Hartsville Highway. Time estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours over the southeast slopes of the North Carolina Blue Ridge. The storms intensified and consolidated into a single, large supercell thunderstorm that moved southward across Upstate South Carolina. Large hail, damaging winds and a brief tornado were produced by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of weak tornado damage was embedded within downburst damage near Lake Greenwood off Amber Hill Circle. Damage was mainly limited to several trees knocked down and some windows blown out of a home due to flying debris. However, some siding was stripped off a couple of homes. Parts of trees also fell on two homes. A pontoon boat was also tossed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures in combination with low relative humidity values, resulted in conditions favorable for wildfire starts in southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Forestry Commission reported a 100 acre wildfire near Shulerville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures in combination with low relative humidity values, resulted in conditions favorable for wildfire starts in southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Forestry Commission reported a 160 acre wildfire 9 miles north-northeast of Hardeeville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated over the mountains of North Carolina during the afternoon hours. As the afternoon progressed, several supercell thunderstorms developed which tracked southeast across the foothills and piedmont along a slow moving surface cold front. Many of the storms moved along the North Carolina, South Carolina border. With unusually steep lapse rates over the region, several of the storms produced large hail. Fortunately the supercells were a little elevated in nature, which kept any tornadoes from forming over South Carolina. Still, hail ranging up to the size of softballs did quite a bit of damage over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported on Walnut St in Rock Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system that had moved across the deep south during previous days, swept across the eastern Carolinas during the afternoon and evening hours. Instability and shear values were highly supportive of super-cell thunderstorms. The result was a large outbreak of severe weather including tornadoes across eastern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF0 tornado 4 miles south-southeast of Andrews, South Carolina. The maximum wind speed was estimated to 80 mph. The path length was almost two-thirds of a mile and the maximum path width was 30 yards. A tornado touched down near two doublewide trailers on Dave McKenzie Drive. The tornado caused damage to the roof deck of one of the trailers and damaged the skirt and swing set of the other trailer. It also knocked down six trees and tossed two aluminum boats across the yard. The tornado then moved into a wooded area before crossing Gallop Lane and lifting just to the east of the roadway. A dozen trees were snapped and uprooted, some of the fallen trees blocked Gallop Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving line of thunderstorms swept across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the late evening hours of the 4th and early morning hours of the 5th. Strong low level gradient winds and the rapid bowing of the line as it crossed the region contributed to widespread straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down, mainly from the Six Mile area east into Pumpkintown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front in combination with a warm and unstable air mass, resulted in scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 64 miles per hour was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west. A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very large oak tree with a diameter in excess of 3 feet and pecan trees were reportedly uprooted in the city of Dillon. A railroad crossbuck and gate were bent in the city of Dillon. Multiple trees and power lines were reportedly down across Dillon County. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF1 tornado touched down in Little Rock, South Carolina. The maximum wind speed was estimated to 105 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep cutoff area of low pressure was located over the Great Lakes with a surface cold front trailing to the south. A humid air mass was in place at the surface with dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s contributing to very low LCLs, as low as 1000 ft. Severe thunderstorms developed during the morning as height falls and a developing low level jet accelerated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded straight-line winds to 65 mph destroyed a metal carport on Union School Road and blew it 75 yards downwind. A wooden fence was also destroyed and some siding was blown off a house on Union School Road. A large tree fell onto Shiloh Church of God on East Friendfield Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

A powerful storm system that had moved across the deep south during previous days, swept across the eastern Carolinas during the afternoon and evening hours. Instability and shear values were highly supportive of super-cell thunderstorms. The result was a large outbreak of severe weather including tornadoes across eastern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF1 tornado touched down 7 miles east-southeast of Andrews, South Carolina. The maximum wind speed was estimated to 110 mph. The path length was a little less than one-half of a mile and the maximum path width was 60 yards. The tornado touched down west of Indian Hut Road and then crossed the roadway before lifting east of the roadway. There were more than 400 trees snapped on both sides of Indian Hut Road. The road was blocked by dozens of trees before it was finally cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system that had moved across the deep south during previous days, swept across the eastern Carolinas during the afternoon and evening hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF1 tornado touched down 11 miles east of Andrews, South Carolina. The maximum wind speed was estimated to 105 mph. The path length was a half mile and the maximum path width was 60 yards. The tornado touched down west of Johnson Road and caused significant damage to over 50 large trees which were either snapped or uprooted. A roof was ripped off a small shed and another small shed was rolled over. Damage also occurred to a pickup truck. the tornado moved across Johnson Road and then lifted east of the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded a microburst with winds to 70 mph occurred along a small portion of Rambo Lane. The microburst had a path length of one-quarter of a mile and a maximum path width of 80 yards. Two dozen trees were either snapped or uprooted. The microburst also caused minor damage to the skirt and siding of a nearby doublewide trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west. A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted on Main Street. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF0 tornado briefly touched down near the Georgetown-Williamsburg County line in southwest Georgetown County, South Carolina. The tornado moved across Saints Delight Road and then lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west. A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell onto a mobile home on Main Street. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms entered the mountains of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the late afternoon hours. The line intensified and expanded northward as it moved out of the mountains. An environment of high shear and moderate instability supported several strong to severe storms within the line. Most the severe weather was the result of straight line winds, though there were a few reports of large hail. One QLCS tornado was spawned along a strong bowing segment over the upstate. The storms crossed the foothills and western piedmont during the early evening hours, a little ways ahead of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on highway 252 about 10 miles west of Laurens, Strickland Ave and on Bethel Church Rd about 6 miles southwest of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms entered the mountains of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the late afternoon hours. The line intensified and expanded northward as it moved out of the mountains. An environment of high shear and moderate instability supported several strong to severe storms within the line. Most the severe weather was the result of straight line winds, though there were a few reports of large hail. One QLCS tornado was spawned along a strong bowing segment over the upstate. The storms crossed the foothills and western piedmont during the early evening hours, a little ways ahead of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two sheds were blown over and some shingles were torn off a residence on Dayton Dr.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of wind damage extended from the Glenmont Lane, Ledgemont Lane area, to Miller Rd, Smith Hines Rd, I-385, Pilgrim Rd and Kincade Rd. A very short gustnado path of about 50 yards in length and 50 yards in width was found embedded within the downburst damage in the Glenmont Lane area. Damage along the path was mainly confined to downed trees. However, some siding was torn off a few homes while others experienced minor roof damage, mainly associated with the gustnado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave trough lifted out of the Gulf Coast states and across the Carolinas during the evening. The low level wind field ahead of this feature was impressive with winds at 1500 feet in excess of 50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down on Manning Highway. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms entered the mountains of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the late afternoon hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey found a short tornado path embedded within a larger area of downburst winds. The track began along Goodwin Rd, just east of Goodwin Bridge Rd. Numerous trees were snapped off or uprooted toward the northeast along the east/southeast oriented damage path that roughly paralleled Goodwin Rd. One tree fell on and damaged a mobile home. The underpinning was blown out from underneath and a metal awning tossed from another mobile home. The tornado crossed highway 25 before lifting in the Boswell Rd area just before its intersection with highway 414.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in the northeast Gulf of Mexico and tracked eastward across the northern Florida peninsula, then northeastward off the southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina coast.  Meanwhile, a shallow cold airmass remained in place in the lee of the Appalachians by high pressure north of the area, allowing a continued supply of cold and dry air at the surface.  The warm temperatures well above ground level and freezing or sub-freezing temperatures at ground level, resulted in freezing rain and ice accumulation across much of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a thin glaze of ice due to freezing rain on roads in Walterboro, South Carolina.  The icing on the roads resulted in a few vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow associated with a Gulf Coast storm system spread from south to north across the upstate and northwest piedmont of South Carolina between midnight and 3 am. By sunrise, most areas had received from 4 to 6 inches of snow. The snow became lighter after sunrise, but continued into the afternoon hours. Total snowfall ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the region. During the early afternoon, precipitation changed to light freezing rain and continued into the evening hours. This added as much as a tenth of an inch of ice to the heavy snowfall totals. Persistent cold temperatures ensured that many roads remained snow-packed or ice covered for several days. Some schools and businesses remained closed for as much as 5 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved through the Gulf of Mexico and across northern Florida producing snow, sleet and freezing rain across the southeast U.S.  Heavy snow fell across the Midlands of South Carolina with accumulations ranging from 1 inch in the southeast portions with up to 10 inches in the northwest portions.  The snow changed to sleet and then to freezing rain and drizzle with ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch.  Power outages were reported in the southern area of the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches with the heaviest amounts in the south portion of the county.  Freezing rain followed the snow with ice accumulations of 1/4 inch.  A few power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disorganized line of thunderstorms moved across western South Carolina during the late afternoon and evening hours. The storms produced a few damaging wind gusts, particularly over the northwest piedmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in the northeast Gulf of Mexico and tracked eastward across the northern Florida peninsula, then northeastward off the southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina coast.  Meanwhile, a shallow cold airmass remained in place in the lee of the Appalachians by high pressure north of the area, allowing a continued supply of cold and dry air at the surface.  The warm temperatures well above ground level and freezing or sub-freezing temperatures at ground level, resulted in freezing rain and ice accumulation across much of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Amateur Radio Operator reported 0.50 inches of ice accumulation on vehicles in the Kane Bay subdivision near the entrance to Route 176.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in the northeast Gulf of Mexico and tracked eastward across the northern Florida peninsula, then northeastward off the southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina coast.  Meanwhile, a shallow cold airmass remained in place in the lee of the Appalachians by high pressure north of the area, allowing a continued supply of cold and dry air at the surface.  The warm temperatures well above ground level and freezing or sub-freezing temperatures at ground level, resulted in freezing rain and ice accumulation across much of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Allendale county Emergency Manager reported Storm Total ice accumulations of 0.50 to 1.00 inches across the county along with numerous downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mature squall line crossed from eastern Tennessee, into the mountains of North Carolina, during the early afternoon hours. While the shear was fairly weak, the strong cold pool and moderate instability ahead of the line supported quite a bit of wind damage as the storms moved out across the northern upstate of South Carolina. Other more discrete storms developed over the western part of the upstate. These storms also produced damaging winds and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Red Hill Lane in Landrum and at Cannon Ford|Rd and Country Estates Rd in Fingerville. Trees were also blown down at Cooley|Springs School Rd and McSwain Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed out ahead of a cold front and pushed across the forecast area from west to east starting in the early evening hours. This was a strongly forced QLCS associated with a vigorous mid and upper level shortwave trough in an environment defined by high shear and relatively low surface based CAPE. This line of thunderstorms eventually pushed offshore over the coastal waters and continued to produce strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 1 tree down on Highway 3 in Estill, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures aloft made the environment conducive for hail and one storm in Horry County did produce large hail during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An environment of moderate wind shear and moderate instability overspread the western Carolinas during the afternoon hours, with bulk shear values over 40 kts. A line of severe storms developed over eastern Tennessee during the late afternoon hours and swept across the central and southwestern mountains. The storms held together as they crossed the mountains, and the line quickly moved across the South Carolina upstate, causing considerable wind damage and a few areas of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down from Cross Keys to the Santuc community.

Clusters of thunderstorms developed near a long-wave trough east of South Carolina. Thunderstorms became strong as they entered an unstable environment featuring MLCAPE between 2000-2500 J/KG and DCAPE over 1000 J/KG. The sea breeze and outflow boundaries enhanced deep moist updrafts and supported multicell thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported 2 trees down in Wescott Plantation.

Clusters of thunderstorms developed ahead of a mid-level trough and very quickly turned severe as they increased in coverage during the mid and late afternoon.  Several storm mergers and storm-boundary interactions were observed through radar interrogation. The environment was very unstable and soundings indicated a pronounced inverted-v signature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels was reported near Socastee High School.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down from the Hickory Grove area, across Sharon and to McConnells. Hickory Grove was especially hard hit, with several homes damaged or destroyed by falling trees. In addition, much of the roof was blown off a chicken house near Hickory Grove. Several other smaller structures received minor damage as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms developed along the lee side of the Appalachians during the middle of the afternoon. These thunderstorms then moved into the Pee Dee Region during the evening. Wind damage was associated with the gust front which preceded the actual thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near the Sister Sister Grocery on East Old Camden Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upstream convection organized into a MCS/QLCS early in the evening before it shifted southeast and affected a large portion of southeast South Carolina during late night through early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 6 inch diameter tree branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of mainly pulse severe storms developing within an unseasonably hot and humid airmass, a cold front crossed the region being more organized severe weather to upstate South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along highway 11 near Boones Creek Rd. Strong winds also damaged the roof of a home in the same area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Stateline Rd at highway 274 and another tree was downed at highway 49 and highway 274.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave shifted through the North Carolina mountains and Midlands before shifting towards the Atlantic coastal waters along the Southeast coast. Mixed-layer CAPE values between 3500-4000 J/KG supported strong thunderstorm development during late evening and early overnight hours as the shortwave passed over portions of southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense heating led to a very unstable airmass across the area. The environment was conducive for downbursts. Afternoon storms eventually organized into an MCS during the evening. The MCS pushed slowly offshore and weakened during the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down at the intersection of North Center Road and Patrick Highway. Two mobile homes sustained damage from the fallen trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down from near Lowrys in the northern part of the county, across the city of Chester, to areas southeast of Chester. Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees.  In addition, six metal turkey houses were damaged at a farm near Lowrys, killing numerous turkeys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed during afternoon/evening hours as a descending synoptic scale front and an inland progressing sea breeze enhanced moisture convergence over South Carolina. Thunderstorms continued into early night hours as they interacted with meso-scale boundaries from previous thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on Morgandollar Road near Coosawhatchie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An environment of moderate wind shear and moderate instability overspread the western Carolinas during the afternoon hours, with bulk shear values over 40 kts. A line of severe storms developed over eastern Tennessee during the late afternoon hours and swept across the central and southwestern mountains. The storms held together as they crossed the mountains, and the line quickly moved across the South Carolina upstate, causing considerable wind damage and a few areas of large hail.

Dozens of trees were blown down on the|property of the Greenville-Spartanburg Airport alone, where a gust of 59 mph was measured by official NWS equipment. Two hangers at the airport were also damaged. In addition, several travel trailers were overturned at a dealership between the airport and I-85. Shingles and siding were also ripped off some homes, particularly in the Reidville area. Some mobile home under-skirting was also blown out near Woodruff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms merged into one huge cluster of thunderstorms. This cluster moved south and into Marlboro County during the late evening hours. The thunderstorms generated a cold pool which helped to sustain them as they propagated toward the coast overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reportedly down on Community Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered pulse type convection developed within a weak shear and moderate CAPE environment over portions of southern South Carolina and Southeast Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorms that intersected an outflow boundary as well as the inland moving sea breeze became strong, primarily producing damaging wind gusts. Storms first became severe over South Carolina before developing southward in Georgia where the outflow boundary and sea breeze intersected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down on US-17A between Round O and Cottageville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms developed ahead of a mid-level trough and very quickly turned severe as they increased in coverage during the mid and late afternoon.  Several storm mergers and storm-boundary interactions were observed through radar interrogation. The environment was very unstable and soundings indicated a pronounced inverted-v signature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot temperatures and high levels of humidity, resulted in heat indices which met or surpassed Excessive Heat Warning criteria across portions of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat index of 115 degrees was measured by the AWOS at the East Cooper Regional Airport, 12 miles northeast of Charleston, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a digging mid and upper level long wave trough provided sufficient uplift in an unstable environment. Once thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, boundary interactions and downdraft CAPES between 800 to 1000 j/kg provided a favorable environment for several strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported one tree down on Friendship Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper trough ahead of an approaching cold front provided sufficient uplift for shower and thunderstorm development over southeast South Carolina. Numerous pulse type thunderstorms became strong as they encountered mixed layer CAPE values between 2500 to 3500 j/kg and downdraft CAPE values exceeding 1000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on a power line near the intersection of Combahee Road and Hendersonville Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough was a catalyst for convection just west of the forecast area in the late afternoon. The convection became organized and progressed toward the coast through the evening, with numerous boundary interactions taking place while this occurred. The airmass was unstable with steep low-level lapse rates and favorable DCAPE values supportive of strong downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported numerous trees down on Bacons Bridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough was a catalyst for convection just west of the forecast area in the late afternoon. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper trough ahead of an approaching cold front provided sufficient uplift for shower and thunderstorm development over southeast South Carolina. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in the southwest part of Chester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid atmosphere was present over southeast South Carolina before convective initiation occurred along a residual outflow boundary over the area and a lee side trough inland. Sbcapes near 5,000 j/kg, LIs around -9C and dcapes near 1300 j/kg supported strong pulse type thunderstorms with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees and power lines down in Beaufort, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed west of Interstate 95 and evolved into a cluster. The cluster of thunderstorms reached the Pee Dee Region during the evening and intensified as it interacted with the seabreeze. The convection tracked along a dewpoint and instability gradient. Wet microbursts did produce several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Elberry Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the southern part of upstate South Carolina during the afternoon hours along a weak stationary front. A few of the storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down at Lockhart Dr and North 2nd St in Lockhart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal convection intensified in bands across north Florida and the Carolinas ahead of a strong surface low in Alabama. This low was the direct remnant of Tropical Storm Lee. Mesoscale boundaries and the sea breeze enhanced thunderstorms which raced northward and formed some bowing segments which produced wind damage in southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Allendale county 911 Dispatch center reported trees and power lines down on Bluff Road, 4 miles west-northwest of Barton, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed over the Upstate from late morning into the early evening hours. The storms in the late afternoon produced a couple areas of significant wind damage over the western part of the upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and large limbs were blown down along Smith Grove Rd. Dozens of trees were blown down in the area around Johnson Rd and Zion Church Rd, with some trees on houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate wind shear and moderate instability developed over the region ahead of a cold front. Scattered multicell storms developed over western South Carolina. The storms produced damaging straight line winds and large hail.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along the lee trough over the central upstate of South Carolina during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms became severe, producing small pockets of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down northeast of Iva.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along the lee trough over the central upstate of South Carolina during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms became severe, producing small pockets of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in Buffalo. Multiple large limbs were also blown down on Mill St.

Mesoscale boundaries and the sea breeze enhanced thunderstorms which raced northward and formed some bowing segments which produced wind damage in southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a tree down on Becks Ferry Road near Highway 321, three miles north of Purrysburg, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Irene passed by to the east of the South Carolina coastline on August 26, 2011, producing tropical storm force wind gusts, beach erosion, high surf and rip currents across portions of southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media relayed a report of High Surf associated with Hurricane Irene undermining several homes on Sullivans Island, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough was a catalyst for convection just west of the forecast area in the late afternoon. The convection became organized and progressed toward the coast through the evening, with numerous boundary interactions taking place while this occurred. The airmass was unstable with steep low-level lapse rates and favorable DCAPE values supportive of strong downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a roof ripped off a trailer and several pecan trees uprooted, near Barkerville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough was a catalyst for convection just west of the forecast area in the late afternoon. The convection became organized and progressed toward the coast through the evening, with numerous boundary interactions taking place while this occurred. The airmass was unstable with steep low-level lapse rates and favorable DCAPE values supportive of strong downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a gust near 60 mph in Bluffton, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered severe thunderstorms developed over portions of the Midlands and produced some wind damage, mainly taking down trees and some powerlines.  Training thunderstorms also produced 2 to 4 inches of rain over downtown Columbia causing flash flooding in Maxcy Park, Five Points, and USC.  Several vehicles were submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported dime size hail in Whitmire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered severe thunderstorms developed over portions of the Midlands and produced some wind damage, mainly taking down trees and some powerlines.  Training thunderstorms also produced 2 to 4 inches of rain over downtown Columbia causing flash flooding in Maxcy Park, Five Points, and USC.  Several vehicles were submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from training thunderstorms produced flash flooding in downtown Columbia in the Maxcy Park, Five Points, and USC areas.  Water levels rose to as high as 4 to 6 feet in some areas submerging a few vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several trees down on Ebenezer Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported widespread structural damage.  Outstorage buildings were blown apart, roof damage to a house and a front porch moved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The State newspaper reported several trees down in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west. A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reportedly down on South Main Street. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours over the southeast slopes of the North Carolina Blue Ridge. The storms intensified and consolidated into a single, large supercell thunderstorm that moved southward across Upstate South Carolina. Large hail, damaging winds and a brief tornado were produced by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms produced wind damage and hail across the forecast area of central South Carolina.  Wind damage was mainly confined to trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Truck driver on I-26 at MM 137 reported a wind gusts of 65 mph and nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms produced wind damage and hail across the forecast area of central South Carolina.  Wind damage was mainly confined to trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Post Office reported large limbs and a few trees down in the area of McKnight and Atkins roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated over the mountains of North Carolina during the afternoon hours. As the afternoon progressed, several supercell thunderstorms developed which tracked southeast across the foothills and piedmont along a slow moving surface cold front. Many of the storms moved along the North Carolina, South Carolina border. With unusually steep lapse rates over the region, several of the storms produced large hail. Fortunately the supercells were a little elevated in nature, which kept any tornadoes from forming over South Carolina. Still, hail ranging up to the size of softballs did quite a bit of damage over the region.

A powerful storm system that had moved across the deep south during previous days, swept across the eastern Carolinas during the afternoon and evening hours. Instability and shear values were highly supportive of super-cell thunderstorms. The result was a large outbreak of severe weather including tornadoes across eastern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reportedly down on Mont Clare Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multicell severe storm developed over western South Carolina during the late afternoon hours. The storm produced large, damaging hail as it moved south across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball size fell in a nearly continuous swath from 5 miles southwest of Greer, across the Five Forks and Mauldin areas, ending in Fountain Inn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across the area igniting scattered thunderstorms. These storms thrived along an inland trough and briefly pulsed to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree reported down on West Hampton Pointe Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep cutoff area of low pressure was located over the Great Lakes with a surface cold front trailing to the south. A humid air mass was in place at the surface with dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s contributing to very low LCLs, as low as 1000 ft. Severe thunderstorms developed during the morning as height falls and a developing low level jet accelerated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded straight-line winds to 60 mph downed several large tree limbs along South Old River Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front in combination with an upper level disturbance and a warm unstable air mass, resulted in scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail up to ping pong ball size along Foster Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved rapidly east to southeast from the mountains through the Midlands of South Carolina taking down numerous trees and powerlines and producing some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees down in the Plum Branch area.  The McCormick Messenger Newspaper also reported a boat shed destroyed on Landam Creek Road in Plum Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance embedded within a northwest steering flow interacted with a surface frontal boundary and the sea breeze within a strongly sheared environment featuring impressive convective parameters, resulting in scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported golf ball size hail, 4 miles east-northeast of Awendaw, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms developed near a long-wave trough east of South Carolina. Thunderstorms became strong as they entered an unstable environment featuring MLCAPE between 2000-2500 J/KG and DCAPE over 1000 J/KG. The sea breeze and outflow boundaries enhanced deep moist updrafts and supported multicell thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several trees down along roadways in the Ravenel area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse storms consolidated into a few multicell clusters late in the afternoon and produced areas of wind damage and large hail as they moved across the upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Cherokee Falls Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak impulse rotated around a large closed low located across the lower Great Lakes. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening. A few of these storms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on N. Martintown Rd. near US Hwy 378.

A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree reportedly fell onto a house on Georgetown Street. The time was estimated based on radar data.

A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Choppee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms developed along the lee side of the Appalachians during the middle of the afternoon. These thunderstorms then moved into the Pee Dee Region during the evening. Wind damage was associated with the gust front which preceded the actual thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near the intersection of North 5th Street and Patrick Highway. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damage winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees down on Racoon road between Manville and St Charles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front and moved through the area producing some large hail and wind damage.  Most of the damage was due to trees falling, some on homes or vehicles.  Three small tornadoes also touched down in the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported power outages across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of tropical cyclone ���Lee��� moved toward the region, increasing wind shear and instability resulted in a few low topped, rotating thunderstorms. Despite the storms having strong mid-level rotation on radar, only one of them went on to produce a tornado over the North Carolina piedmont. A little straight-line wind damage was also reported over the upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in Tega Cay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in combination with modest instability and strong deep layer shear, resulted in scattered strong to severe thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Public reported half dollar size hail broke a car window on Shell Point Road in Beaufort, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex developed in the middle and upper northwest flow over the region. This complex moved southeast across the Northern and Central Midlands overnight. The system produced numerous reports of downed trees and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County EM reported trees down across Northern Fairfield County from White Oak to Mitford.

A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reportedly down on Tom Cribb Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak impulse rotated around a large closed low located across the lower Great Lakes. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening. A few of these storms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blocking road near US 176 and Mt. Bethel Garmany Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disorganized line of thunderstorms moved into the northwest piedmont of South Carolina, producing some wind damage. Other discrete storms developed over the central and western upstate, which also caused some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down on Haynes Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed in association with an upper low over the southeast states. Some of the storms produced hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail covered the ground to a depth of 2 inches at Tokeena Rd and highway 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms affected the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of the thunderstorms were severe, producing large hail and damaging straight line wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the Calhoun Falls area, with at least one tree on a home. Trees and limbs were blown down across the reminder of the county as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours over the southeast slopes of the North Carolina Blue Ridge. The storms intensified and consolidated into a single, large supercell thunderstorm that moved southward across Upstate South Carolina. Large hail, damaging winds and a brief tornado were produced by the storm.

A fast moving line of thunderstorms swept across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the late evening hours of the 4th and early morning hours of the 5th. Strong low level gradient winds and the rapid bowing of the line as it crossed the region contributed to widespread straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across the county, especially in the Callison area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the region ahead of a weak frontal boundary. A few of these storms became severe and also produced locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down along River Road near Lake Wateree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms drifted out of the North Carolina mountains, and consolidated into a large storm that moved across much of Oconee County. The storm produced hail up to the size of baseballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported very large hail ranging from 2-inches in diameter to baseball size in the|Mountain Rest area, including along Whetstone Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced wind damage, power outages, and large hail across the area.  Power outages lasted up to 12 hours in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local meteorologist reported marble to golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west. A long-lived and extensive squall line, well ahead of the front, moved into the area during the early morning hours and then off the coast shortly after sunrise. A southwest low-level jet in excess of 60 kt was transporting mid 60 degree dewpoints into the area. The magnitude of the shear and wind fields were enough to overcome the time of day and very limited instability. The result was widespread wind damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and power lines were reportedly down on Westover Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough in combination with the inland pushing sea breeze in a moderate to strongly unstable air mass, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Fire Department employee reported a tree and several tree limbs down on Highway 45 near Alvin, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms across the Midlands produced a few severe weather events, mostly taking down trees across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbia Police reported multiple roads and intersections flooded and closed due to high water levels of 2 to 5 feet.  These included Main and Whaley, Gervais and Laurens, Santee and Harden, and Rosewood and Assembly.  A vehicle was submerged at Key Road and George Rogers Blvd.  The gage at Pickens St. on the Rocky Branch Creek reached 8.7 ft.  Flood stage is 7.2 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved rapidly east to southeast from the mountains through the Midlands of South Carolina taking down numerous trees and powerlines and producing some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured at 53 knots...or 61 mph...at McEntire Air National Guard Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved over the upper level ridge and convection that developed west of the Carolinas and Georgia moved east southeast across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility Company reported large tree limbs down and power lines down on Fed Rivers Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initially fired along subtle inland boundaries, then occasionally pulsed to severe levels as they interacted with a sea breeze and numerous complex outflow boundaries from other thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported one tree down along Mighty Cougar Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms produced some large hail and wind damage in the CSRA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a couple of trees down on SR 283 near Upper Miller road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved rapidly east to southeast from the mountains through the Midlands of South Carolina taking down numerous trees and powerlines and producing some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported  several trees and powerlines down around Lexington.
